allopack.title: States of Matter — Same Stuff, Different Behavior
allopack.author: AlloFlow community catalog (authored by Claude, reviewed by A. Pomeranz)
allopack.standards: NGSS 5-PS1-1 (develop a model to describe that matter is made of particles too small to be seen); NGSS 5-PS1-2 (measure and graph quantities to provide evidence that the total weight of matter is conserved when substances are heated, cooled, or mixed); CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.4.3 (explain events, procedures, or concepts in a scientific text)

data.body:
**Due:** (your teacher will tell you)

Ice, water, and steam look like three different things. They are the exact same stuff. The only difference is how fast the tiny pieces are moving.

1. Read **The Dance Floor** — the whole lesson is in that one comparison.
2. Learn the words in the **Glossary**, then win the **Matching** game.
3. Keep the **Three States** chart nearby.
4. Sort the cards in **Solid, Liquid, or Gas?**
5. Take the quiz. Question 3 is the one that catches almost everybody.

One thing to watch for all lesson: nothing is created or destroyed when matter changes state. It only rearranges. ❄️💧☁️

objectives.label: I watched a state change happen at home and described it

history.title: The Dance Floor
history.data:
## The Dance Floor

Everything around you is made of pieces far too small to see, called **particles**. What makes ice different from water is not what the particles are. It is how they are moving.

Picture a crowded dance floor.

In a **solid**, everyone is packed shoulder to shoulder and only shivering in place. Nobody moves past anybody. That is why a solid keeps its own shape — an ice cube stays a cube whether you put it in a bowl, a cup, or your hand.

In a **liquid**, the music starts and people begin sliding past each other, still touching but no longer locked. That is why water flows and takes the shape of whatever holds it, but the amount stays the same. Pour a cup of water into a bowl and you still have a cup of water.

In a **gas**, everyone breaks loose and bounces around the whole room, far apart and moving fast. That is why a gas spreads out to fill any container completely. Steam does not sit in a puddle in the air.

What moves particles between these states is **energy** — usually heat. Add heat and the particles move faster. Take heat away and they slow down.

Heat ice and it **melts** into water. Keep heating and it **evaporates** (or boils) into water vapor. Cool that vapor and it **condenses** back into liquid — that is the fog on a bathroom mirror. Cool the liquid further and it **freezes** back into a solid.

Here is the part that trips people up. When the pot boils, the cloudy stuff you see rising is not steam in the scientific sense — real water vapor is completely invisible. What you see is tiny liquid droplets that formed when hot vapor hit cooler air and condensed. Look right at the spout and you will notice a small clear gap before the cloud starts. That gap is the actual gas.

And nothing disappears. A melted ice cube weighs exactly what it weighed frozen. A puddle that dries up did not vanish — its particles spread into the air. Matter changes how it behaves, not how much of it there is. That rule holds everywhere, which is why scientists trust it enough to build on it.

data.def: Anything that takes up space and has mass.
One of the tiny pieces that all matter is made of, too small to see.
Matter whose particles are packed tightly and hold their own shape.
Matter whose particles slide past each other, taking the shape of the container.
Matter whose particles are far apart and spread to fill any space.
To change from a solid into a liquid by adding heat.
To change from a liquid into a solid by removing heat.
To change from a liquid into a gas.
To change from a gas back into a liquid.
What makes particles move — adding heat adds energy.
How much space something takes up.

sections.label: SOLID
bullets: Particles packed tight, shivering in place
sections.iconPrompt: tightly packed dots in a neat grid

sections.label: LIQUID
sections.iconPrompt: loose dots flowing in a curved container

sections.label: GAS
sections.iconPrompt: widely scattered dots with motion arrows

sections.label: CHANGES
bullets: All of these can be reversed
sections.iconPrompt: a thermometer with arrows up and down

sections.label: THE BIG RULE
bullets: A melted ice cube weighs the same
Matter rearranges, it does not vanish
sections.iconPrompt: a balance scale holding ice on one side and water on the other

history.title: Solid, Liquid, or Gas?

items.content: Sand in a bucket (each grain is solid)

history.meta: 3 states • 9 items • sand and honey start arguments

history.title: Matter Check

questions.question: What is the real difference between ice and liquid water?
options: They are made of completely different particles
Ice has more particles packed inside it
How fast the particles are moving
Water has energy and ice has none at all
questions.correctAnswer: How fast the particles are moving

questions.question: Why does a gas fill its whole container while a liquid does not?
options: Gas is lighter than the container
Liquids are attracted to the bottom by magnetism
Gas particles are much larger than liquid particles
Gas particles are far apart and bounce around the entire space
questions.correctAnswer: Gas particles are far apart and bounce around the entire space

questions.question: You see a white cloud above a boiling pot. What is it?
options: Tiny liquid droplets that formed when hot vapor cooled
Pure water vapor, which is naturally cloudy and white
Smoke rising from the hot metal of the pot
Ordinary air being pushed up and out of the pot
questions.correctAnswer: Tiny liquid droplets that formed when hot vapor cooled

questions.question: An ice cube melts completely in a sealed container. What happens to its mass?
options: It gets lighter, because solid ice weighs more than liquid water
It stays exactly the same
It gets heavier because water is denser
The mass disappears along with the ice cube's shape

questions.question: Water droplets appear on the outside of a cold glass on a hot day. What happened?
options: Water leaked out through tiny holes in the glass
The glass melted slightly and dripped
The ice inside evaporated through the glass
Water vapor in the air condensed on the cold surface
questions.correctAnswer: Water vapor in the air condensed on the cold surface

questions.question: A puddle on the sidewalk disappears after a sunny afternoon. Explain where the water went using the word particles.
questions.expectedAnswer: The Sun added energy, so the water particles moved faster and escaped into the air as invisible water vapor. The water did not disappear — it evaporated and its particles spread out into the air.

questions.question: Use the dance-floor comparison to explain the difference between a solid and a liquid to a younger student.
questions.expectedAnswer: In a solid, people are packed shoulder to shoulder and only shiver in place, so the shape stays the same. In a liquid, they slide past each other while still touching, so it flows and takes the shape of its container.

reflections.text: Describe one state change you have seen at home this week.
Which state would you rather be a particle in — solid, liquid, or gas? Explain your choice.

history.meta: 5 MCQ + 2 short answer • Q3 is the steam trap

data.question: Is there a fourth state of matter? 🔥
data.answer: Yes — plasma, where particles get so much energy that their pieces come apart. Lightning, neon signs, and the Sun are all plasma. Most of the visible universe is plasma, which makes solid, liquid, and gas the unusual ones.

data.question: Is glass a very slow liquid?
data.answer: A famous claim, and it is basically a myth. Old windows are thicker at the bottom because of how glass used to be made, not because it flowed. Glass at room temperature behaves as a solid.

data.question: Why does ice float when most solids sink in their own liquid? 🧊
data.answer: Water is strange. When it freezes, the particles lock into a pattern with extra space in it, so ice is less dense than water. If it worked the normal way, lakes would freeze from the bottom up and life in them would be very different.

data.question: Can something be two states at once?
data.answer: At exactly the melting or boiling point, yes — ice water sitting at zero degrees Celsius is solid and liquid together while the change is happening.

data.question: What is dry ice? ❄️
data.answer: Frozen carbon dioxide. It skips the liquid stage entirely and goes straight from solid to gas, which is called sublimation. That is why it makes fog without ever making a puddle.

data.question: Does air really weigh something?
data.answer: It does. The air in an average classroom weighs roughly as much as a large adult. You do not feel it because it presses on you evenly from every direction.
